skill.name: gws-shared
skill.description: gws CLI: Shared patterns for authentication, global flags, and output formatting.
skill.summary: Reference documentation for the Google Workspace CLI tool
skill.author: googleworkspace

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives from markdown documentation code blocks. The skill contains only reference documentation for the gws CLI tool with no executable code or actual security risks.

content.value_statement: This skill provides comprehensive reference documentation for the gws CLI tool. It helps you authenticate, use global flags, and follow proper CLI syntax for Google Workspace operations.
actual_capabilities: Documents authentication methods for gws CLI including OAuth and service accounts
Provides reference for global flags like format output and dry-run mode
Explains CLI syntax structure for service-resource-method commands
Lists method flags for parameters, JSON body, file uploads, and pagination
Documents security rules for safe CLI operations
Includes shell tips for proper command quoting in zsh and bash
limitations: This is a documentation-only skill and does not execute commands
Requires the gws binary to be installed separately on your system
Does not provide interactive command execution or error handling
Reference material only - does not validate or test commands

use_cases.title: CLI Quick Reference
use_cases.description: Look up gws CLI syntax, flags, and authentication methods while working with Google Workspace APIs from the command line.

use_cases.title: Authentication Setup Guide
use_cases.description: Learn how to authenticate with gws using browser-based OAuth or service account credentials for automated workflows.

use_cases.title: Best Practices Reference
use_cases.description: Access security rules and shell tips to ensure safe and proper usage of the gws CLI tool in production environments.
use_cases.target_user: Security-conscious teams automating Google Workspace tasks

prompt_templates.title: Basic Authentication Help
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I authenticate with the gws CLI using a service account?
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up automated Google Workspace access

prompt_templates.title: Command Syntax Lookup
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the gws CLI syntax for listing Google Drive files with JSON output format
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a command to query Google Drive

prompt_templates.title: Pagination and Output
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I use gws to fetch all Gmail messages with automatic pagination and save as CSV?
prompt_templates.scenario: Exporting large datasets from Google Workspace APIs

prompt_templates.title: Safe Operations with Dry-Run
prompt_templates.prompt: What is the recommended way to test a destructive gws command before executing it?
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning a batch delete operation safely

output_examples.input: How do I authenticate with gws?
output_examples.output: You can authenticate with gws using two methods: 1) Browser-based OAuth with 'gws auth login' for interactive sessions, or 2) Service account by setting the GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS environment variable to point to your key.json file.

output_examples.input: Show me the global flags available in gws
output_examples.output: gws supports these global flags: --format (output as json/table/yaml/csv), --dry-run (validate without API calls), and --sanitize (screen responses through Model Armor for content safety).

output_examples.input: How do I handle shell special characters in gws commands?
output_examples.output: For zsh, use double quotes when ranges contain '!' (e.g., --range "Sheet1!A1:D10"). For JSON parameters, wrap in single quotes to preserve inner double quotes (e.g., --params '{"pageSize": 5}').

best_practices: Always use the --dry-run flag first to validate destructive operations before executing them
Prefer --format json for programmatic processing and --format table for human-readable output
Use --sanitize flag when handling PII or content that requires safety screening
anti_patterns: Do not output secrets or API keys directly in command output - use secure credential management
Avoid using single quotes for sheet ranges in zsh as the '!' character triggers history expansion
Never run write or delete commands without confirmation unless in automated scripts with proper safeguards

faq.question: Do I need to install the gws binary separately?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill requires the gws binary to be installed on your system and available in your PATH. Refer to the project README for installation options.

faq.question: What is the difference between --params and --json flags?
faq.answer: Use --params for URL/query parameters and --json for request body data. Both accept JSON-formatted strings wrapped in single quotes to preserve inner double quotes.

faq.question: How do I handle large result sets with gws?
faq.answer: Use the --page-all flag for automatic pagination with NDJSON output. Control pagination with --page-limit to set max pages and --page-delay to add delays between requests.

faq.question: Can I test commands without making API calls?
faq.answer: Yes, use the --dry-run flag to validate your command structure and parameters locally without executing actual API calls. This is essential for testing destructive operations.

faq.question: Why does my sheet range command fail in zsh?
faq.answer: Zsh interprets the '!' character in ranges like 'Sheet1!A1' as history expansion. Use double quotes instead of single quotes: --range "Sheet1!A1:D10".

faq.question: How do I report bugs or request features?
faq.answer: Search existing issues first at github.com/googleworkspace/cli/issues. If no match exists, open a new issue with details rather than creating duplicates.
